title: Russia’s Antarctic posture provokes – but don’t invoke
gist: Claire Young argues that although Russian Antarctic ground stations plausibly support GPS/Beidou jamming and the new Sarmat 'Satan-II' ICBM, invoking the Antarctic Treaty's non-militarisation provisions against Russia would be a mistake — the support is modest, legally ambiguous under the Treaty's silence on space, and challenging it risks unravelling the entire Antarctic Treaty System's inspection and environmental regime for little strategic gain.

insights.claim: American and European analysts believe Russia has managed to briefly jam US GPS and the Chinese equivalent, Beidou, over continental-sized areas during the past seven years, apparently using a constellation of early-warning satellites flying in a Molniya orbit that maximises time over Russia and the United States.
insights.speaker: Claire Young
insights.so_what: A significant, if analyst-attributed, capability claim — continental-scale GPS/Beidou jamming has direct implications for military and civilian navigation-dependent systems in affected regions.
insights.locator: American and European analysts believe Russia

insights.claim: Russia plans to deploy a new intercontinental ballistic missile, the RS-28 Sarmat (dubbed 'Satan-II' in the West), by the end of 2026, designed to fly long range including over southern polar regions to attack from a direction less defended by the US, reportedly carrying a Fractional Orbital Bombardment System that can place a nuclear weapon in low orbit before it glides to target.
insights.speaker: Claire Young
insights.so_what: A specific, dated capability milestone (Sarmat deployment by end of 2026) with a described southern-polar attack vector and FOBS capability — a concrete item for strategic-arms and missile-defense watchers to track.
insights.locator: Russia has also announced it will deploy

insights.claim: Both the Sarmat missile and Russia's early-warning satellites could get confirmation or refinement of their location and timing information from Russian ground stations in Antarctica, and the Deputy Director of the Russian Institute of Arctic and Antarctic Research has said Russia needs 'a spot in the Russkaya station's area as when one of our satellites fly over there it's not visible,' as Roscosmos upgrades the old Russkaya station in unclaimed Antarctica.
insights.speaker: Claire Young
insights.so_what: Names the specific facility (Russkaya station) and agency (Roscosmos) behind the dual-use Antarctic upgrade — the concrete asset to monitor for further military-relevant investment.
insights.quote: a spot in the Russkaya station's area as when one of our satellites fly over there it's not visible
insights.locator: The Deputy Director of the Russian Institute

insights.claim: The author's core framework is that despite real dual-use support from Russian Antarctic stations, the Antarctic Treaty's non-militarisation provisions are not being violated, for three reasons: the Treaty is silent on space overflight, the support is modest and not unique under a de minimis principle, and challenging it risks damaging the whole Treaty System.
insights.speaker: Claire Young
insights.so_what: Sets out the analytical structure for evaluating any future Antarctic militarisation dispute, useful for assessing how credible a legal challenge against Russia (or any state) would actually be.
insights.locator: But the Treaty provisions are not being violated

insights.claim: The Treaty is silent on space overflight — even though the first polar-orbiting satellite was launched by the United States the same year the Treaty was signed — and aerial observation for Treaty inspections is explicitly allowed, undercutting any claim that satellite-related ground support automatically breaches non-militarisation rules.
insights.speaker: Claire Young
insights.so_what: A specific legal gap (Treaty silence on space overflight) that shapes how any future dispute over satellite-support infrastructure in Antarctica would likely be adjudicated.
insights.locator: The first is that the Treaty is silent

insights.claim: The support Antarctic facilities provide to Russia's missile and satellite capabilities is likely modest and not unique, since Russia's GLONASS system has ground stations in Latin America and one in South Africa that could probably provide similar location and timing data, and several Russian naval vessels could do the same job.
insights.speaker: Claire Young
insights.so_what: Undercuts the strategic significance of the Antarctic stations specifically — alternative non-Antarctic assets (GLONASS ground stations, naval vessels) could substitute, weakening the case that Antarctica is a militarily indispensable node.
insights.locator: The second is that the support provided

insights.claim: Publicly calling out Russia's Antarctic ground-station operations could easily damage the entire Antarctic Treaty System — including the non-militarisation regime, the right of any state to inspect stations, vessels and aircraft to verify non-militarisation, and the Treaty's environmental rules — meaning the cost of triggering a dispute would outweigh any benefit.
insights.speaker: Claire Young
insights.so_what: The article's central policy recommendation: restraint is strategically rational because the inspection and verification regime the Treaty enables is itself the main tool for monitoring dual-use activity — breaking it to punish Russia would be self-defeating.
insights.locator: The third reason is that were these Antarctic

insights.claim: There is no bright line between scientific and military uses in Antarctica, as civilian scientific information such as weather reports, hydrographic mapping, and telescopes tracking space debris can suddenly become militarily valuable in a crisis or war, making any attempt to itemise what counts as science versus military data likely fruitless — illustrated by the fact the Antarctic community could not even agree at its last meeting to make the Emperor penguin a 'specially protected species.'
insights.speaker: Claire Young
insights.so_what: Illustrates just how low the bar for consensus is within the Antarctic Treaty community, implying any attempt to renegotiate militarisation definitions would likely fail entirely, reinforcing the case for restraint.
insights.locator: At its last meeting the Antarctic community could not even agree

insights.claim: The author recommends that any assessment of potential military uses of Antarctic equipment should weigh whether the facility is unique in a state's global architecture, how quickly and securely information can be transmitted, and how much it improves military capability — though much of this can only be fully assessed with classified information, so governments should proactively explain why they are not concerned, to avoid unnecessary securitisation of Antarctic narratives.
insights.speaker: Claire Young
insights.so_what: A concrete policy prescription for governments (proactive transparency over escalatory accusation) to prevent Antarctic dual-use concerns from spiralling into a broader security dispute that reduces cooperation on issues like krill regulation and the 2048 mining-ban review.
insights.locator: If any action is to be taken
